title: English with Kyle
topic: Comparisons: not quite as…as / not nearly as…as / just about

sections.title: 1. Warm-Up Translations
sections.content:
**Direct & Indirect Object Pronouns**

When a pronoun replaces the object, the word order changes — the preposition **to** is required:

| Spanish | English |
|---|---|
| Explícamelo una vez más | Explain it to me one more time *(not "explain me it")* |
| Kyle le está dando el libro a Pepe | Kyle is giving Pepe the book → He is giving **it** to **him** |

---

**Translation practice from class**

| Spanish | English |
|---|---|
| Fue mucho más fácil que la última vez | It was much easier than the last time |
| ¿Cuánto tardó? | How long did it take you? *(subject = "it")* |
| Él estaba mejor preparado que yo | He was better prepared than me / than I was |
| Vino el fontanero para mirarlo | The plumber came to look at it |
| Se estaban quejando de la falta de un plan concreto | They were complaining about the lack of a concrete plan |
| Pero vieron esto, ¿no? | But they saw this, **didn't they**? |

> **Vocabulary note:** *plumber* has a **silent B** — another example: *crumbs*.

sections.title: 2. Question Tags
sections.content:
Take the **auxiliary verb**, flip it (affirmative → negative or negative → affirmative), then repeat the subject pronoun.

| Sentence | Tag |
|---|---|
| It is important… | …isn't it? |
| You like coffee… | …don't you? |
| He likes fish… | …doesn't he? |
| He was here yesterday… | …wasn't he? |
| They can't fix it… | …can they? |
| But they saw this… | …didn't they? |

> With the verb **to be**, it acts as its own auxiliary — no extra verb needed.
>
> In Canadian English, people informally say **"eh?"** instead of a proper tag (not standard, but common).

sections.title: 3. Picture Vocabulary
sections.content:
Verbs and phrases practised from images:

| Image | Expression |
|---|---|
| Man looking in mirror | He's **shaving** *(we don't say "shaving himself")* |
| Two men greeting | They're **bowing** / They're **shaking hands** |
| Man with camera | He's **taking a photo** / **taking a picture** · The camera is **flashing** |
| Sunrise/sunset scene | The sun is **rising** / **setting** over the desert |
| Crowd entering a store | They're **rushing into** the store / They're **crashing the door** *(Black Friday)* |
| People in the rain | They're **taking shelter** under a palm leaf |
| Person with gift | She/he is **wrapping a gift** / **wrapping a present** · **wrapping paper** |

**Extra vocabulary:**
- **A sale / on sale** — something sold at a reduced price
- **Door crash deal** — a special offer only for the first people through the door (Black Friday term)
- **Mixed reviews** — some people liked it, some didn't *(taquilla = box office)*

sections.title: 4. Comparatives with as…as  ★ Main topic
sections.content:
### Basic structure: **as … as** / **not as … as**

*tan … como / no tan … como*

- Valencia is **not as big as** Madrid
- I'm **not as tall as** my brother
- English is **not as difficult as** Chinese

---

### 1. Not quite as … as — *small gap, close comparison*

> Implies the two things are **almost the same** but one falls slightly short.

- It's **not quite as** salty **as** last time *(casi igual de salado)*
- The museum wasn't **quite as** interesting as we hoped
- The exam wasn't **quite as** difficult as I thought it would be
- Barcelona isn't **quite as** big as Madrid
- My house isn't **quite as** big as my brother's
- The film is **not quite as** good as the book
- His second book is **not quite as** interesting as the first
- Málaga's beaches aren't **quite as** long as Cádiz's

---

### 2. Not nearly as … as — *large gap, big difference*

> Implies there is a **significant** difference — one thing is much less than the other.

- I'm **not nearly as** fast as Usain Bolt *(con mucha diferencia)*
- Trujillo isn't **nearly as** big as Madrid
- This cover isn't **nearly as** good as the Beatles original
- His desk isn't **nearly as** untidy as hers
- The film isn't **nearly as** good as the book
- My hometown is **not nearly as** crowded as Madrid
- It doesn't have **nearly as much** traffic as Madrid *(countable/uncountable applies: as much / as many)*

---

### Quick comparison

| Expression | Spanish equivalent | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| not quite as … as | no tan … como *(casi)* | Small — almost equal |
| not nearly as … as | ni con mucho tan … como | Large — clearly less |

---

### 3. Just about as … as — *almost equal, barely reaches*

> Like saying *casi* — it's right there, very close.

- I'm **just about as** good as him at tennis *(casi tan bueno como él)*
- My brother is taller but I'm **just about as** tall
- My mother's cooking is **just about** the best in the world
- My grandmother was **just about** the most cheerful person I've ever known
- It's **just about** the best burger in town

---

### 4. Just about to — *on the verge of doing something*

> Means *a punto de hacer algo* — you were very close to doing it but didn't.

- I was **just about to** call you *(a punto de llamarte)*
- He was **just about to** open the window when it started pouring
- That's what I was **just about to** say
- I was **just about to** go out when you called me
- I was just about to warn you when the ceiling fell

> **Note:** this is different from *just about + adjective* above — same words, different meaning depending on context.

sections.title: 5. Weather Vocabulary
sections.content:
| Verb | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| to **pour** / it's **pouring** | diluviar | *It started pouring rain.* |
| to **drizzle** / it's **drizzling** | chispear, lloviznar | *I love Paris when it's drizzling.* |
| to **sprinkle** / it's **sprinkling** | llovizna muy ligera | *It's just sprinkling — no need for an umbrella.* |

> **Sprinkler** — a device that sprinkles water; used in buildings as a fire safety system.
>
> *Drizzle* is also used in cooking: *drizzle olive oil over the salad* = echar un chorrito de aceite.

sections.title: 6. Vocabulary Notes
sections.content:
| Word / phrase | Notes |
|---|---|
| **plumber** | Silent B — pronounced *"plummer"*. Other silent-B words: *crumbs*, *thumb*, *climb* |
| **a cover** | A cover version of a song *(versión)* |
| **box office** | Taquilla de cine — *box office numbers / box office records* |
| **mixed reviews** | Some people liked it, some didn't |
| **cheerful** | Risueño, alegre — from *cheer* (to send good wishes) |
| **to rush** | Ir con prisa, correr — *they rushed into the store* |
| **to take shelter** | Refugiarse — *they took shelter from the rain* |
| **lack** (noun) | Falta, carencia — *the lack of a concrete plan* |
| **just about** | Casi / a punto de — two different uses depending on context |

qs.q: "Explícamelo una vez más" → correct English:
opts: Explain me it one more time
Explain it to me one more time
Explain to me it one more time
qs.ans: Explain it to me one more time

qs.q: "Kyle is giving Pepe the book." With pronouns this becomes:
opts: He is giving it to him
He is giving to him it
qs.ans: He is giving it to him

qs.q: "Fue mucho más fácil que la última vez" → correct English:
opts: It was much more easy than the last time
It was much easier that the last time
It was much easier than the last time
qs.ans: It was much easier than the last time

qs.q: "Se estaban quejando de la falta de un plan concreto" → correct English:
opts: They were complaining about the miss of a concrete plan
They were complaining about the lack of a concrete plan
They were complaining for the lack of a concrete plan
qs.ans: They were complaining about the lack of a concrete plan

qs.q: "Vino el fontanero para mirarlo" → correct English:
opts: The plumber came for look at it
The plumber came to looking at it
The plumber came to look at it
qs.ans: The plumber came to look at it

qs.q: The word "plumber" has a silent letter. Which one?
"It is important, ___"
"He likes fish, ___"
"He was here yesterday, ___"
"They can't fix it, ___"
"You like coffee, ___"
With the verb "to be", how do you form the tag? "It is cold, ___"
A man looks in the mirror while cleaning his face with a razor. He is:
Two people lower their heads to greet each other in the Japanese style. They are:
A man points a camera and presses the button. He is:
On Black Friday a crowd runs as fast as possible to enter the store. They are:
People hide from heavy rain under a large palm leaf. They are:
Someone folds paper around a Christmas present. They are:
"Barcelona es algo más pequeña que Madrid, pero la diferencia no es grande." Best translation:
opts: Barcelona isn't nearly as big as Madrid
Barcelona isn't quite as big as Madrid
Barcelona is just about as big as Madrid
qs.ans: Barcelona isn't quite as big as Madrid

qs.q: "Trujillo es muchísimo más pequeño que Madrid." Best translation:
opts: Trujillo isn't quite as big as Madrid
Trujillo is just about as big as Madrid
Trujillo isn't nearly as big as Madrid
qs.ans: Trujillo isn't nearly as big as Madrid

qs.q: "La película es mucho peor que el libro." Best translation:
opts: The film isn't quite as good as the book
The film is just about as good as the book
The film isn't nearly as good as the book
qs.ans: The film isn't nearly as good as the book

qs.q: "Casi soy tan bueno como él al tenis (muy poca diferencia)." Best translation:
opts: I'm not quite as good as him at tennis
I'm not nearly as good as him at tennis
I'm just about as good as him at tennis
qs.ans: I'm just about as good as him at tennis

qs.q: "A punto de llamarte cuando tú llamaste." Best translation:
opts: I was just about calling you when you called
I was just to call you when you called
I was just about to call you when you called
qs.ans: I was just about to call you when you called

qs.q: Which structure implies the LARGEST difference between two things?
"Está diluviando." How do you say this in English?
"Está chispeando / lloviendo muy poco." How do you say this in English?
"Apenas cae agua — llovizna ligerísima." How do you say this in English?
A device on a building's ceiling that releases water to fight fires is called a:
"Drizzle" is also used in cooking. What does it mean there?
opts: To pour a small amount of liquid (e.g. olive oil) over food
qs.ans: To pour a small amount of liquid (e.g. olive oil) over food

qs.q: "Taquilla del cine" in English is:
"Risueño, alegre" in English is:
"Ir con prisa, correr" in English is:
A version of a song performed by a different artist is called a:
"La falta / carencia de algo" in English is:
What does "mixed reviews" mean?
opts: Some people liked it and some did not
qs.ans: Some people liked it and some did not

title: English with Kyle
topic: Business Letter Translation / Past Continuous / Phrasal Verbs

sections.title: 1. Warm-Up: Past Continuous
sections.content:
Used for actions **in progress** when something else happened (interruption) or for **simultaneous** past actions.

Structure: **was/were + verb-ing**

| Spanish | English |
|---|---|
| ¿Qué pasó cuando venías aquí? | What happened when you were coming here? |
| Le vi cuando iba a clase | I saw him when he was going to class |
| ¿Qué hacías cuando les viste? | What were you doing when you saw them? |
| ¿A dónde ibas cuando tuviste el accidente? | Where were you going when you had the accident? |
| Bajaba de su coche cuando la bomba estalló | He was getting out of his car when the bomb went off |
| Pensaba en ti cuando me llamaste | I was thinking about you when you called me |
| Cuando me di cuenta del error, ya se iba | When I realized my mistake, he was already leaving |

> **“What did it happen?”** ✗ — When asking for the **subject**, no auxiliary is needed: **“What happened?”** ✓

---

### By the time

*Para cuando* — one thing finished before the other was complete.

- **By the time** I realized my mistake, he was already leaving.
- **By the time** the bus came, we were already late.
- **By the time** our flight arrived, the taxis had already gone.

sections.title: 2. Phrasal Verbs: Get & Blow
sections.content:
### Get into / Get out of — enclosed spaces

Use with **cars, taxis, elevators**:

- **Get into** the car · **Get out of** the car
- **Get into** a taxi · **Get out of** an elevator

### Get on / Get off — large vehicles

Use with **buses, planes, trains**:

- **Get on** the bus · **Get off** the bus
- **Get on** the plane · **Get off** the train

> Things you **get into**, you **get out of**. Things you **get on**, you **get off**.

---

### Blow / Go Off / Put Out

| Expression | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| The bomb **went off** | La bomba estalló | *The bomb went off at midnight.* |
| The building **blew up** | El edificio voló por los aires | *The explosion blew up the building.* |
| **Blow out** a candle | Apagar soplando | *Blow out your birthday candles.* |
| **Put out** a fire | Apagar un incendio | *Firefighters put out the fire.* |

> A bomb **goes off**. A building **blows up**. Candles are **blown out**. Fires are **put out**.

sections.title: 3. Think About / Think Of / Think Up
sections.content:
Three related but distinct expressions:

| Expression | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Think about** | Pasar un rato con algo en la cabeza | *I was thinking about you / about hamburgers.* |
| **Think of** | In the middle — slight implication of invention | *I thought of a solution last night while washing the dishes.* |
| **Think up** | Pure invention / idear | *We need to think up a new recipe for the greatest burger ever.* |

> **Think about** = just having something in your mind, no invention implied.
>
> **Think up** = purely creative — *we need to think up a plan.*
>
> **Think of** = somewhere in between; the idea *appeared* — *I can’t think of the word right now.*

sections.title: 4. Notice vs. Realize
sections.content:
| Expression | Spanish | Type |
|---|---|---|
| **To notice** | Fijarse — con los sentidos | Sensory awareness — less cerebral |
| **To realize** | Darse cuenta | Mental processing — more cerebral |

**Example sequence:**
> *I noticed a strange smell* → sensory, external
> *Then I realized my neighbor was smoking.* → mental processing, understanding the cause

- **Notice** = becoming aware **through the senses** (sight, smell, sound)
- **Realize** = **understanding** what something means, processing consequences

> Notice is less cerebral — it’s external. Realize involves insightfulness — you process what it *means*.

sections.title: 5. Business Letter: Formal English  ★ Main topic
sections.content:
A complaint letter translated from Spanish, practising key formal English phrases.

---

### Key phrases and vocabulary

| Spanish | English |
|---|---|
| A quien corresponda | **To whom it may concern** |
| Defectuoso | **Defective** / **faulty** |
| Cliente (compra producto) | **Customer** |
| Cliente (compra servicio) | **Client** |
| Dar por sentado | **To take for granted** |
| Más de 30 años | **Over** 30 years *(more natural than “more than”)* |
| Probar algo nuevo | **To try out** |
| Intentar | **To try** |
| Compensar | **To compensate** / **to make up for** |
| Como si | **As if** |
| Arañazos | **Scratches** |
| Adjunto dentro del sobre | **Enclosed** |
| Adjunto al email | **Attached** |
| El culpable (un objeto) | **The offending** — *the offending blades* |

---

### Grammar notes

**Who vs. Whom:** Grammar books say use *whom* for objects, but in real life almost nobody does — except **“To whom it may concern.”**

**“Will can” does not exist →** use **“will be able to”:**
> *You’ll be able to imagine my anger.*

**Enclosed vs. Attached:**
- *Enclosed* = inside the envelope / embedded in the email body.
- *Attached* = a separate file annexed to the email (like a PDF).

**Customer vs. Client:**
- *Customer* = someone who buys a **product**.
- *Client* = someone who buys a **service**.

**Try vs. Try out:**
- *Try* = intentar (*I tried to climb the wall but I couldn’t.*)
- *Try out* = probar algo nuevo (*I decided to try out your new razor.*)

**Defective vs. Faulty:** both mean *defectuoso*. *Faulty* comes from *fault* (falta/defecto).

sections.title: 6. Vocabulary Notes
sections.content:
| Word / phrase | Notes |
|---|---|
| **to take for granted** | Dar por sentado — *I started to take it for granted.* |
| **to make up for** | Compensar — *How do you intend to make up for this?* |
| **over** (+ number) | Más de — *over 30 years, over 100 people* |
| **the offending X** | El X culpable (for objects, not people) — *the offending blades* |
| **as if** | Como si — *It looked as if it had been attacked.* |
| **by the time** | Para cuando — *By the time I arrived, it was over.* |
| **to blow out** | Apagar soplando — *blow out the candles* |
| **to put out** | Apagar (fuego) — *put out the fire* |
| **faulty / defective** | Defectuoso — *a faulty blade / a defective product* |
| **faithful / loyal** | Fiel — *a faithful customer for over 30 years* |
| **scratches** | Arañazos |
| **to rush** | Ir con prisa — *they rushed into the store* |

qs.q: Why is "What did it happen when you were coming here?" wrong?
opts: When asking for the subject, no auxiliary is needed
qs.ans: When asking for the subject, no auxiliary is needed

qs.q: "Le vi cuando iba a clase." →
opts: I saw him when he went to class
I saw him when he was going to class
I was seeing him when he was going to class
qs.ans: I saw him when he was going to class

qs.q: "¿Qué hacías cuando les viste?" →
opts: What did you do when you saw them?
What have you done when you saw them?
What were you doing when you saw them?
qs.ans: What were you doing when you saw them?

qs.q: The past continuous is used for:
opts: A completed action in the past
An action in progress being interrupted, or simultaneous past actions
qs.ans: An action in progress being interrupted, or simultaneous past actions

qs.q: "By the time I realized my mistake, he was already leaving." "By the time" means:
opts: Para cuando — by the point that
qs.ans: Para cuando — by the point that

qs.q: "Pensaba en ti cuando me llamaste." →
opts: I thought about you when you called me
I was thinking about you when you called me
I was thinking about you when you were calling me
qs.ans: I was thinking about you when you called me

qs.q: "Bajarse del autobús" →
"Subirse al coche" →
"La bomba estalló" →
"Apagar una vela soplando" →
"El edificio voló por los aires" →
Which sentence is correct?
opts: She got out of the car
She got down of the car
qs.ans: She got out of the car

tests.title: Test 3 · Think About / Think Of / Think Up

qs.q: Which expression implies pure invention or creativity?
"Think up" implies:
opts: Just having something in your mind

qs.q: "Se nos ocurrió una solución." (the idea appeared) →
"Tenemos que inventar un nuevo plan." →
opts: We need to think about a new plan
We need to think up a new plan
We need to think of a new plan
qs.ans: We need to think up a new plan

qs.q: "Think about" implies:
opts: Just having something in your mind — no invention implied
qs.ans: Just having something in your mind — no invention implied

qs.q: "Me fijé en que había un olor extraño." →
opts: I realized there was a strange smell

qs.q: "Me di cuenta de que mi vecino estaba fumando." →
opts: I noticed my neighbor was smoking
I realized my neighbor was smoking
I recognized my neighbor was smoking
qs.ans: I realized my neighbor was smoking

qs.q: "Notice" is mainly associated with:
Kyle described "realize" as:
opts: More cerebral — understanding consequences and meaning
qs.ans: More cerebral — understanding consequences and meaning

qs.q: "I noticed Kyle's microphone sounded different, then I ___ he had changed it."
"A quien corresponda" →
A person who buys a product is a ___; a person who buys a service is a ___:
"Dar por sentado" →
"Probar algo nuevo para ver qué tal" →
Photos inside an envelope are ___; a PDF sent with an email is ___:
"Will can" does not exist. The correct form is:
"Compensar por algo" →
"Defectuoso" →
"La cuchilla culpable" (object, not person) →
"Como si hubiera sido atacado" →
opts: As if it had been attacked
qs.ans: As if it had been attacked

qs.q: "Para cuando llegé, ya había terminado" →
opts: When I arrived, it was over
By the time I arrived, it was over
At the time I arrived, it was over
qs.ans: By the time I arrived, it was over

qs.q: "Cliente fiel" →

title: English with Kyle
topic: Mastering the Interrogative / The History of Coca-Cola

sections.title: 1. Warm-Up Translations
sections.content:
| Spanish | English |
|---|---|
| Deben de habérsele pegado las sábanas | He must have overslept |
| Hemos llegado a un acuerdo consensuado | We've reached a compromise / an agreement |
| Las dos empresas se fusionaron | The two companies merged |
| Se tardaron meses en negociar la fusión | It took them months to negotiate the merger |
| Ha habido despidos masivos en ese sector | There have been massive layoffs in the sector |
| Lo único que me interesa es conservar mi empleo | The only thing I'm interested in is keeping my job |

---

### Oversleep vs. Sleep in

| Expression | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **To oversleep** | Quedarse dormido — problem, unintentional | *I overslept and missed the bus.* |
| **To sleep in** | Dormir más — intentional, no problem | *Tomorrow I can sleep in — it's Sunday.* |

---

### Compromiso — False Friend ⚠️

| Spanish | English |
|---|---|
| Tengo un **compromiso** (obligación) | I have a **commitment** *(not "a compromise")* |
| Hemos llegado a un **acuerdo** / punto medio | We've reached a **compromise** |

> *Compromise* = a mutual agreement where both sides give a little. *Commitment* = an obligation or promise.

---

### To merge / A merger

- **To merge** = fusionarse → *The two companies merged.*
- **A merger** = la fusión (de empresas) → *It took months to negotiate the merger.*

sections.title: 2. Work vs. Job / Layoff vs. Fired
sections.content:
### Work (uncountable) vs. Job (countable)

| Word | Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Work** | Uncountable noun / verb | *He loves his work. She works hard.* |
| **Job** | Countable noun | *He has two jobs.* |

- *He does good **work**.*
- *He has a good **job**.*
- *My friend has two **jobs** — one in an office, one in a restaurant.*

> **Works** (countable) = *obra* — literary or artistic:
> *The complete **works** of Shakespeare · **works** of poetry · a **work** of art*

---

### Layoff vs. Fired

| Expression | Spanish | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Laid off** | Despedido sin culpa propia | Economic reasons, redundancy — not your fault |
| **Fired** | Despedido con causa | Your fault — misconduct, theft, poor performance |

- *He got **laid off** because the economy went bad.*
- *He got **fired** because he was stealing from the company.*

---

### Industry vs. Sector

Both are correct, but **industry** is more common in everyday English:
- *I work in the financial **industry**.*
- *There have been massive layoffs in the tech **industry**.*

sections.title: 3. Mastering the Interrogative  ★ Main topic
sections.content:
Form a question by identifying the **question word**, then: **question word + auxiliary + subject + verb?**

> Give the statement → identify what information is being asked → build the question.

---

### Coca-Cola facts → questions

| Statement | Question |
|---|---|
| Coca-Cola was invented **in 1886**. | **When** was Coca-Cola invented? |
| The inventor was **John Pemberton**. | **Who** was the inventor of Coca-Cola? |
| The headquarters is **in Atlanta**. | **Where** is the headquarters of Coca-Cola? |
| The main ingredient is **carbonated water**. | **What** is the main ingredient in Coca-Cola? |
| The logo is **red and white**. | **What color** is the famous Coca-Cola logo? |
| Coca-Cola was first sold **at a pharmacy**. | **Where** was Coca-Cola first sold? |
| The company was founded by **Asa Candler**. | **Who** was the Coca-Cola company founded by? |
| Coca-Cola sponsors **the Olympic Games**. | **What** does Coca-Cola sponsor? |
| Coca-Cola owns the brand **Fanta**. | **What brand** does Coca-Cola own? |
| Coke had a giant failure with **New Coke**. | **What** did Coca-Cola have a giant failure with? |
| They introduced the contoured bottle **in 1915**. | **When** did they introduce the contoured bottle? |
| New Coke was introduced **in 1985**. | **When** did Coca-Cola introduce New Coke? |
| Coca-Cola Orange was introduced **in 2007**. | **When** did Coca-Cola introduce Coca-Cola Orange? |

sections.title: 4. Coca-Cola History & Vocabulary
sections.content:
### Key facts

- Invented in **1886** by **John Pemberton**, a pharmacist in **Atlanta**.
- Originally sold at **pharmacies** — carbonated water was believed to aid digestion.
- The Coca-Cola *company* was founded by **Asa Candler**, who built the bottling and distribution empire.
- The iconic **contoured bottle** was introduced in **1915**.
- **New Coke** launched in **1985** — a huge failure. After public backlash, the original was renamed **Coca-Cola Classic**.
- **Coca-Cola Orange** introduced in **2007**.
- Owns many brands including **Fanta** and various bottled water brands. Sponsors the **Olympic Games**.

---

### Names for soft drinks by region

| Region | Word |
|---|---|
| Canada | **Pop** |
| USA — Northeast (New York) | **Soda** |
| USA — South (Florida) | **Coke** *(used for any soft drink)* |
| Formal / universal | **Soft drink** |

> In the US South: *"What kind of Coke do you want?" "A Sprite."*

---

### Sweetener differences

| Region | Sweetener in regular Coke |
|---|---|
| USA | High fructose corn syrup |
| Europe | Sugar (beet sugar) |
| Mexico | Cane sugar *(Mexican Coke is popular in the US for its flavor)* |

sections.title: 5. Speculate & Forecast
sections.content:
### To speculate — especular

> To form opinions without firm evidence; to invest in hopes of profit.

| Example |
|---|
| I speculated on the stock market last year. |
| They have speculated about the reasons for the decision. |
| Are you speculating when you say that? |

> **Pronunciation:** unvoiced S — say **"speculate"**, not **"a-speculate"**.

---

### To forecast — prever

> To predict future events. Can be a **verb** or a **noun**.

| Example |
|---|
| They forecasted sales of over 3 million euros. |
| Economists have forecasted a rise in the stock market. |
| I'm forecasting a fall in sales this year. |
| Is it possible to correctly forecast a recession? |

> **A weather forecast** (noun) = el pronóstico del tiempo.

> **Prever** conjugation note: *prevé*, not *prevere*.

sections.title: 6. Vocabulary Notes
sections.content:
| Word / phrase | Notes |
|---|---|
| **to oversleep** | Quedarse dormido (problema) — *I overslept and missed the meeting.* |
| **to sleep in** | Dormir más (intencionado) — *I can sleep in tomorrow.* |
| **to merge** | Fusionarse — *The two companies merged.* |
| **a merger** | La fusión (de empresas) |
| **laid off** | Despedido sin culpa propia — causas económicas |
| **fired** | Despedido con causa |
| **a commitment** | Un compromiso (obligación) ⚠️ false friend: ≠ *compromise* |
| **a compromise** | Un acuerdo de punto medio |
| **to speculate** | Especular — bolsa, opiniones sin certeza |
| **to forecast** | Prever — también noun: *a weather forecast* |
| **soft drink** | Refresco (término formal/universal) |
| **pop** | Refresco (Canadá) |
| **soda** | Refresco (EE.UU. noreste) |
| **carbonated water** | Agua con gas/carbonatada |
| **the works of X** | Las obras de X (literarias/artísticas) |
| **industry** | Sector — más común que 'sector' en inglés coloquial |

qs.q: "Deben de habérsele pegado las sábanas." →
What is the difference between "oversleep" and "sleep in"?
opts: Oversleep = quedarse dormido (problem); sleep in = dormir más (intentional)
Sleep in = quedarse dormido (problem); oversleep = dormir más (intentional)
qs.ans: Oversleep = quedarse dormido (problem); sleep in = dormir más (intentional)

qs.q: "Las dos empresas se fusionaron." →
"La fusión de empresas" →
"Tengo un compromiso." ⚠️ False friend — correct translation:
"Hemos llegado a un acuerdo (punto medio)." →

tests.title: Test 2 · Work vs. Job / Layoff vs. Fired

qs.q: Which word is countable?
"Tiene dos trabajos." →
"The complete ___ of Shakespeare" (obras literarias)
"He was ___ because the company downsized." (no fue su culpa)
"She got ___ for stealing from the company."
Kyle said ___ is more common than "sector" in everyday English.
"Coca-Cola was invented in 1886." → asking about WHEN:
"The headquarters of Coca-Cola is in Atlanta." → asking about WHERE:
opts: What is the headquarters of Coca-Cola?
Where is the headquarters of Coca-Cola?
Who is the headquarters of Coca-Cola?
qs.ans: Where is the headquarters of Coca-Cola?

qs.q: "John Pemberton invented Coca-Cola." → asking about WHO:
opts: When did John Pemberton invent Coca-Cola?

qs.q: "Coca-Cola owns the brand Fanta." → asking about WHAT brand:
"They introduced the contoured bottle in 1915." → asking about WHEN:
opts: Where did they introduce the contoured bottle?
When did they introduce the contoured bottle?
qs.ans: When did they introduce the contoured bottle?

qs.q: "Coca-Cola introduced New Coke in 1985." → asking about WHAT was introduced:
opts: When did Coca-Cola introduce New Coke?

qs.q: Who invented Coca-Cola?
Where was Coca-Cola first sold?
The iconic contoured bottle was introduced in:
New Coke was launched in ___ and was a huge failure.
In Canada, a soft drink is called:
Mexican Coke is popular in the US because it is sweetened with:
"Especularon sobre las razones de la decisión." →
opts: They forecast the reasons for the decision
They speculated about the reasons for the decision
They forecasted the reasons for the decision
qs.ans: They speculated about the reasons for the decision

qs.q: "To forecast" can be used as:
opts: Both a verb and a noun
qs.ans: Both a verb and a noun

qs.q: Kyle said the pronunciation of "speculate" starts with:
opts: An extra vowel before the S (a-speculate)

qs.q: "Especular en bolsa" →
opts: To forecast on the stock market
To speculate on the stock market
To merge on the stock market
qs.ans: To speculate on the stock market

qs.q: "El pronóstico del tiempo" →
"Compromiso" meaning obligation — correct English:
"Refresco" — the formal/universal English term:
"Ha habido despidos masivos." →
"Las obras de Shakespeare" →
"Trabajo en el sector financiero." (more natural in English)
opts: I work in the financial sector
I work in the financial industry
qs.ans: I work in the financial industry

qs.q: "Se tardaron meses en negociar la fusión." →
opts: They took months to negotiate the fusion
It took them months to negotiate the compromise
It took them months to negotiate the merger
qs.ans: It took them months to negotiate the merger
